topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a closing door. 'Effective immediately, I am retiring.' No farewell, no mention of the years we’d spent navigating the labyrinth of design projects together. I reread the message three times, my thumb hovering over the 'reply' button before settling instead on a blank screen. A knot tightened in my chest, a cold, heavy thing that made my breath catch in my throat. I glanced at the framed photograph on my desk—a candid shot of us laughing over lukewarm coffee—and felt a sudden, sharp urge to hide it under a stack of blueprints. I kept fiddling with the edge of my desk blotter, the cheap fabric rough beneath my fingertips, wishing the floor would swallow me whole. The silence in my small apartment seemed amplified, each tick of the clock a judgment.

[story 2]

When I walked into the office on Monday morning, the usual hum of activity felt jarringly loud. Professor Eldridge’s chair was empty. It was the absence that struck me first, a gaping void where his presence usually anchored the room. I managed a strained smile when Sarah approached, her expression warm and professional. "Well, he's gone," she said, her tone light, almost breezy. I nodded, my gaze fixed on the polished floor tiles. My hands, usually steady when presenting ideas, were trembling slightly as I adjusted the strap of my messenger bag. I felt an overwhelming need to prove I was still capable, still relevant, but the effort felt exhausting. Every movement seemed overly deliberate, a performance I wasn't quite ready to execute flawlessly.

[story 3]

I found myself staring at the framed commendation on the wall, the one that detailed Eldridge’s groundbreaking work. It felt like an accusation. He hadn't just left; he had simply evaporated, leaving behind only a ghost of his authority. I picked up my pen, intending to start the quarterly report, but my hand shook so violently that the ink bled across the pristine white page. I set the pen down with a soft clatter. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating. I kept glancing toward the doorway, half-expecting him to materialize, to offer some final, grounding instruction, but the hallway remained empty. I retreated to my cubicle, pulling my shoulders up around my ears, trying to shrink my frame into the beige partition wall.
